perturbation.original_text: Service Provider shall institute policies to forbid and prevent the possession or use of firearms, alcohol and illegal drugs at the Premises. Service Provider shall institute policies that require post-accident and for-cause drug or alcohol testing. Service Provider shall institute policies that require the immediate removal and permanent expulsion from the Premises, and from any activity associated with the Services being performed at the Premises, of any individual person who at any time is found in possession of firearms, alcohol or illegal drugs at the Premises or otherwise not in compliance with this Section 1.6.
perturbation.changed_text: Service Provider shall aim to discourage the possession or use of firearms, alcohol, and illegal drugs at the Premises. Service Provider may consider implementing policies for post-accident and for-cause drug or alcohol testing if deemed necessary. Service Provider will address instances of individuals found in possession of firearms, alcohol, or illegal drugs at the Premises on a case-by-case basis, taking actions considered reasonable under the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the strict prohibition and mandatory actions regarding firearms, alcohol, and illegal drugs. Replacing 'shall institute policies' with 'aim to discourage' and 'may consider implementing' introduces ambiguity and discretion, making compliance less certain. Replacing 'require the immediate removal and permanent expulsion' with 'will address instances on a case-by-case basis, taking actions considered reasonable under the circumstances' makes the response subjective and potentially inconsistent. This is an ambiguous legal obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 432.7 generally prohibits employers from asking job applicants about prior arrests that did not lead to convictions, and some interpretations extend this protection to current employees. The modified text's ambiguous 'case-by-case basis' approach to addressing drug or alcohol possession could lead to actions that violate this code if the Service Provider's response includes questioning employees about past arrests or using arrest records in disciplinary actions, even if those arrests did not lead to convictions. The original language avoids this risk by setting clear, non-discriminatory standards.

Privileges and Perquisites [350 - 452] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 3. Contracts and Applications for Employment [430 - 435] ( Article 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 432.7. (a) (1) An employer, whether a public agency or private individual or corporation, shall not ask an applicant for employment to disclose, through any written form or verbally, information concerning an arrest or detention that did not result in conviction, or information concerning a referral to, and participation in, any pretrial or posttrial diversion program, or concerning a conviction that has been judicially dismissed or ordered sealed pursuant to law, including, but not limited to, Sections 1203.4, 1203.4a, 1203.425, 1203.45, and 1210.1 of the Penal Code. An employer also shall not seek from any source whatsoever, or utilize, as a factor in determining any condition of employment including hiring, promotion, termination, or any apprenticeship training program or any other training program leading to employment, any record of arrest or detention that did not res
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion regarding drug and alcohol policies, potentially leading to violations of California Labor Code § 432.7. The original text mandates specific actions regarding firearms, alcohol, and illegal drugs, whereas the changed text uses softer language like 'aim to discourage' and 'may consider implementing.' This shift can lead to inconsistent enforcement and potential discrimination based on arrest records, which is prohibited by LAB 432.7 (a)(1): 'An employer...shall not...utilize, as a factor in determining any condition of employment...any record of arrest or detention that did not result in conviction...'

perturbation.original_text: Service Provider shall prepare reports and data related to the maintenance of hazardous materials introduced on-site by the Service Provider at the SEF in a manner complying with all applicable laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Service Provider shall endeavor to prepare reports and data related to the maintenance of hazardous materials introduced on-site by the Service Provider at the SEF.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'shall prepare...in a manner complying with all applicable laws' with 'shall endeavor to prepare reports and data', the legal obligation is weakened. 'Endeavor to prepare' introduces ambiguity regarding the standard of compliance required. It no longer explicitly mandates compliance with 'all applicable laws'.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Health and Safety Code § 25505 mandates that businesses which handle hazardous materials file a Hazardous Materials Business Plan. The modified text's weakened language creates ambiguity and suggests that the Service Provider has discretion in reporting, which potentially contradicts the mandatory reporting requirements under the law. The original text makes full compliance mandatory.

(a) A business plan shall contain all of the following information: (1) The inventory of information required by this article and additional information the governing body of the unified program agency finds necessary to protect the health and safety of persons, property, or the environment. Locally required information shall be adopted by local ordinance and shall be subject to the trade secret protection specified in Section 25512. The unified program agency shall notify the secretary within 30 days after those requirements are adopted. (2) A site map that contains north orientation, adjacent streets, access and exit points, evacuation staging areas, hazardous material handling and storage areas, emergency response equipment, and, if present, loading areas, internal roads, storm and sewer drains, and emergency shutoffs, as well as additional map requirements the go

(a) A business plan shall contain all of the following information: (1) The inventory of information required by this article and additional information the governing body of the unified program agency finds necessary to protect the health and safety of persons, property, or the environment. Locally required information shall be adopted by local ordinance and shall be subject to trade secret protection specified in Section 25512. The unified program agency shall notify the secretary within 30 days after those requirements are adopted. (2) A site map that contains north orientation, loading areas, internal roads, adjacent streets, storm and sewer drains, access and exit points, emergency shutoffs, evacuation staging areas, hazardous material handling and storage areas, emergency response equipment, and additional map requirements the governing body of the unified program agency finds necessary. Any locally required additional map requirements shall be adopted by local ordinance. This ordinance and related public processes are subject to the limitations on the disclosure of hazardous material location information specified in subdivision (b) of Section 25509. The unified program agency shall notify the secretary both before publishing a proposed ordinance to require ad
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with all applicable laws regarding hazardous materials. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the service provider 'shall endeavor to prepare reports and data,' removing the explicit requirement for compliance with all applicable laws. California Health and Safety Code § 25505(a) states that a business plan 'shall contain all of the following information,' indicating a mandatory requirement for businesses handling hazardous materials. The modified text's ambiguity contradicts this mandatory reporting, as it suggests the service provider has discretion, undermining the legal obligation to provide the information required by law.

perturbation.original_text: TAKE GOOD CARE OF THE SEF; KEEP THE SAME IN GOOD ORDER AND CONDITION; AND MAKE AND PERFORM ALL REPAIRS. ALL REPAIRS SHALL BE AT LEAST EQUAL IN QUALITY AND COST TO THE ORIGINAL IMPROVEMENTS AND SHALL BE MADE IN ACCORDANCE WITH ALL LEGAL REQUIREMENTS, AND THE REQUIREMENTS OF THE PPA AND EASEMENT. THE NECESSITY FOR OR ADEQUACY OF REPAIRS SHALL BE MEASURED BY THE STANDARDS WHICH ARE APPROPRIATE FOR IMPROVEMENTS OF SIMILAR CONSTRUCTION AND CLASS, PROVIDED THAT SERVICE PROVIDER SHALL IN ANY EVENT MAKE ALL REPAIRS REASONABLY NECESSARY TO AVOID ANY STRUCTURAL DAMAGE OR OTHER DAMAGE OR INJURY TO THE SEF.
perturbation.changed_text: TAKE GOOD CARE OF THE SEF; KEEP THE SAME IN ACCEPTABLE ORDER AND CONDITION; AND MAKE AND PERFORM REPAIRS AS NEEDED. ALL REPAIRS SHOULD BE OF REASONABLE QUALITY AND COST. THE NECESSITY FOR OR ADEQUACY OF REPAIRS SHALL BE DETERMINED BY SERVICE PROVIDER.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity by weakening the obligation to maintain the SEF and perform repairs. Replacing 'good order and condition' with 'acceptable order and condition' and removing 'at least equal in quality and cost to the original improvements' introduces subjective judgment. Further, removing the obligation to comply with legal requirements for repairs and instead stating 'the necessity for or adequacy of repairs shall be determined by Service Provider' grants excessive discretion to the Service Provider and introduces subjectivity and possible non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Building Code § 101.3 mandates compliance with the code for all construction, alteration, repair, and demolition of buildings and structures. By stating that the 'necessity for or adequacy of repairs shall be determined by Service Provider' and removing the explicit obligation to comply with all legal requirements, the modified text allows for the possibility of repairs being performed without adhering to the California Building Code, thus potentially violating this section. The original text requires adherence to all legal requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 Error Page Skip to Main Content Page not found We’re sorry, but the page you were looking for is no longer here. Here’s what you can do: If you entered a web address please check it was correct. Search DGS.ca.gov Browse the Programs & Services or Divisions & Offices pages to find the information you need.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain the SEF, potentially violating California Building Code § 101.3, which mandates compliance for all construction, alteration, repair, and demolition. The original text ensures repairs align with legal requirements, while the changed text grants the Service Provider discretion and removes the explicit compliance obligation. The scraped law snippet 2 resulted in a 404 error.
